attributes.stem:
A 58-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic for her annual diabetes review. She was diagnosed with type 2 diabetes eight years ago. Her medical history includes hypertension and dyslipidaemia. Her medications include metformin, gliclazide, perindopril, and atorvastatin. She works as a school teacher and exercises three times per week.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Her body mass index is 28 kg/m². Her most recent glycated haemoglobin (HbA1c) is 52 mmol/mol (6.9%). You discuss with her a kidney health check, which includes a urine albumin-creatinine ratio check.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate timing for the collection of the required urine sample?
options: Random sample at any time of day
attributes.explanation:
Screening for diabetic nephropathy is an essential component of diabetes management. The urinary albumin-to-creatinine ratio (uACR) is the preferred test for detecting and monitoring diabetic nephropathy, as it provides a quantitative measure of urinary albumin excretion that is standardised to urinary creatinine concentration.

First morning void is the preferred sample for uACR testing. This is because the first morning void provides the most consistent and reliable results with reduced variability. Overnight, while the patient is recumbent, there is less influence from factors that can affect albumin excretion such as exercise, posture, and diurnal variation. This makes the first morning void the most standardised sample for detecting microalbuminuria.

Random sample at any time of day can be used for uACR testing if a first morning void is not available, but it is subject to greater variability due to the factors mentioned above. This can lead to false positive or false negative results, potentially affecting clinical decision-making.

Two hours after breakfast is not specifically recommended for uACR testing. This timing may be influenced by postprandial changes in renal hemodynamics and is not standardised across patients with different dietary habits.

At least 48 hours after exercising is not a recommended guideline for uACR testing. While exercise can transiently increase urinary albumin excretion, the general recommendation is to avoid vigorous exercise in the 24 hours before providing a urine sample, not 48 hours. This option represents an unnecessarily long delay.

24-hour urine collection was historically used to quantify albumin excretion but has largely been replaced by the more convenient and practical uACR test. While a 24-hour collection provides comprehensive information, it is cumbersome for patients, prone to collection errors, and unnecessary for routine screening. It may also result in a cohort of patients not being screened due to an unnecessary burdensome screening test.

It's important to note that a diagnosis of persistent albuminuria (indicating diabetic nephropathy) requires at least two out of three positive uACR tests over a 3-month period. This is because urinary albumin excretion can be transiently elevated due to factors such as urinary tract infections, heart failure exacerbations, acute febrile illnesses, and uncontrolled hypertension or hyperglycemia.
learningPoints: First morning void is the preferred sample for urinary albumin-to-creatinine ratio (uACR) testing due to reduced variability.
Random urine samples for uACR may be affected by exercise, posture, and diurnal variation.
Persistent albuminuria is defined as two out of three positive uACR tests over a 3-6 month period.
references: Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD) Management in Primary Care (5th edition). Kidney Health Australia. 2024. Available from: https://assets.kidney.org.au/resources/KHA-CKD-Handbook-5th-Ed-July2024.pdf

Johnson DW, Jones GR, Mathew TH, Ludlow MJ, Chadban SJ, Usherwood T, Polkinghorne K, Colagiuri S, Jerums G, Macisaac R, Martin H; Australasian Proteinuria Consensus Working Group. Chronic kidney disease and measurement of albuminuria or proteinuria: a position statement. Med J Aust. 2012 Aug 20;197(4):224-5. doi: 10.5694/mja11.11468.

attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
